u1_l1.1.title: Layers of the Earth (Crust, Mantle, Core)

flashcards.definition: Outermost solid layer; continental (thick, granitic) and oceanic (thin, basaltic).
Thick silicate layer between crust and core; contains the partially molten asthenosphere.
Solid iron-nickel sphere at Earth's center; solid despite extreme heat due to enormous pressure.
Rigid outer shell (crust + upper mantle) broken into tectonic plates.
Partially molten zone in the upper mantle that allows tectonic plates to move.
Naturally occurring, inorganic solid with a definite chemical composition and crystalline structure.
Ranking minerals 1 (talc, softest) to 10 (diamond, hardest) based on scratch resistance.
The color of a mineral's powder when rubbed on unglazed porcelain; more reliable than surface color for ID.
The tendency of a mineral to break along flat, predictable planes determined by crystal structure.
How light reflects from a mineral's surface; classified as metallic or non-metallic (vitreous, pearly, earthy).
Formed from cooled and solidified magma (intrusive) or lava (extrusive).
Formed from compacted and cemented sediments, chemical precipitation, or organic remains.
Existing rock transformed by heat, pressure, or chemical processes without fully melting.
The continuous process by which rocks are converted among igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic types.
Parallel alignment of mineral grains in metamorphic rock due to directed pressure, creating banded texture.
Wegener's hypothesis that continents were once joined (Pangaea) and have since drifted apart.
New oceanic crust forms at mid-ocean ridges as magma rises, pushes plates apart, and solidifies.
Where plates move apart; new crust forms (mid-ocean ridges) or continents rift apart.
Where plates collide; denser plate subducts, forming trenches, volcanoes, or mountain ranges.
Where plates slide horizontally past each other; produces earthquakes (e.g., San Andreas Fault).
The point underground where an earthquake originates; the actual location of rock fracture.
The point on Earth's surface directly above the focus; where shaking is typically strongest.
Primary waves; fastest seismic waves; compressional; travel through all states of matter.
Secondary waves; slower than P-waves; shear motion; cannot travel through liquids.
Measures total energy released by an earthquake; each whole number increase = ~32× more energy.
Broad, gently sloping volcano built from fluid basaltic lava flows; relatively gentle eruptions (e.g., Mauna Loa).
Steep-sided volcano built of alternating lava and ash layers; highly explosive eruptions (e.g., Mt. St. Helens).
Resistance to flow; high-silica magma is more viscous and produces more explosive eruptions.
A fast-moving, extremely hot cloud of gas, ash, and rock fragments; the most deadly volcanic hazard.
Scale from 0-8 measuring eruption size based on volume of ejected material.
The process of mountain building, typically by tectonic forces (folding, faulting, and uplift).
Mountains formed by compressional forces folding rock layers upward (e.g., Himalayas, Alps).
Mountains formed when tensional forces cause blocks of rock to be uplifted along normal faults.
An upward (arch-shaped) fold in rock layers; oldest rocks at the center.
A downward (trough-shaped) fold in rock layers; youngest rocks at the center.
A rock containing enough valuable mineral to be mined profitably.
Coal, oil, and natural gas formed from ancient organisms over millions of years; non-renewable energy sources.
Mineral deposit formed when hot, mineral-rich water circulates through rock and deposits minerals in fractures.
A geological structure that stops the upward migration of oil/gas, allowing it to accumulate (anticline, fault, stratigraphic).
The degree of metamorphism of coal: peat → lignite → bituminous → anthracite (increasing carbon content and energy).
Magnitude 9.1 off Japan; triggered a devastating tsunami and the Fukushima nuclear disaster; ~20,000 deaths.
Magnitude 7.0; catastrophic due to poor infrastructure and poverty; ~200,000+ deaths; highlighted preparedness gaps.
Lateral blast eruption in Washington state; 57 deaths; major case study in volcanic monitoring and ecosystem recovery.
Network of ocean buoys and seismic stations that detect tsunamis and alert coastal populations to evacuate.
Planning, infrastructure investment, building codes, early warning systems, and education that reduce natural hazard casualties.
Lowest atmospheric layer (0-12 km); where weather occurs; temperature decreases with altitude.
Layer above troposphere (12-50 km); contains the ozone layer; temperature increases with altitude.
Region in the stratosphere where O₃ absorbs UV radiation, protecting life on Earth.
Third atmospheric layer (50-85 km); coldest layer; where meteors burn up.
Fourth layer (85-600 km); extremely hot molecules but very thin atmosphere; where auroras occur.
Energy transfer via electromagnetic waves; requires no medium (how sunlight reaches Earth through space).
Heat transfer through direct molecular contact; ground heats air touching its surface.
Heat transfer through fluid movement; warm fluid rises, cool fluid sinks, creating circulation cells.
The fraction of incoming solar radiation reflected by a surface; ice/snow have high albedo, dark surfaces low.
The balance between incoming solar radiation and outgoing reflected/emitted radiation; determines Earth's temperature.
Large body of air with relatively uniform temperature and humidity; classified by source region.
Boundary where cold air advances under warm air; produces heavy, brief precipitation and sharp temperature drops.
Boundary where warm air advances over cold air; produces gradual, steady precipitation.
Area of rising air, convergence at the surface, and divergence aloft; associated with clouds and precipitation.
Area of sinking air, divergence at the surface; associated with clear skies and calm weather.
High-altitude, thin, wispy clouds made of ice crystals; often indicate fair weather but can signal approaching fronts.
Towering vertical cloud (thunderstorm cloud); produces heavy rain, lightning, hail, and potentially tornadoes.
Low, flat, layered clouds covering the sky; associated with overcast conditions and steady light precipitation.
Precipitation formation in cold clouds where ice crystals grow at the expense of supercooled water droplets.
Air forced upward by terrain (mountains); causes cloud formation and precipitation on windward side; rain shadow on leeward.
Tropical convection cell: warm air rises at the equator, flows poleward, sinks at ~30° latitude.
Persistent winds blowing from ~30° toward the equator; NE trades in NH, SE trades in SH.
Prevailing winds between 30-60° latitude blowing from west to east; drive mid-latitude weather.
Belt near the equator where trade winds converge; rising air produces heavy tropical rainfall.
System categorizing climates into 5 main groups (A-E) based on temperature and precipitation patterns.
Intense tropical cyclone with sustained winds ≥119 km/h; fueled by warm ocean water (≥26.5°C); has a calm eye.
Violently rotating column of air extending from a thunderstorm to the ground; most destructive winds on Earth.
Abnormal rise in sea level caused by a hurricane's winds pushing water onshore; often the deadliest aspect.
Rates hurricanes Category 1-5 based on sustained wind speed; Category 5 = 252+ km/h.
Rates tornadoes EF0-EF5 based on observed damage; EF5 = total destruction of well-built structures.

u2_l2.7.title: Human Impact on Atmosphere (Pollution, Greenhouse Gases)

flashcards.definition: A pollutant emitted directly into the atmosphere from a source (e.g., CO, SO₂, particulates from burning).
A pollutant formed by chemical reactions in the atmosphere (e.g., ground-level ozone formed from NOx + VOCs + sunlight).
Precipitation with pH below 5.6; formed when SO₂ and NOx react with water vapor; damages ecosystems and structures.
The additional warming caused by human-added greenhouse gases beyond the natural greenhouse effect.
Total greenhouse gas emissions caused by an individual, organization, event, or product, expressed as CO₂ equivalent.
Computer models that solve atmospheric physics equations using current observations to forecast future weather.
Instrument package carried by weather balloon that measures temperature, humidity, pressure, and wind through the atmosphere.
Radar that measures precipitation intensity AND wind velocity/direction by detecting the Doppler shift of returned signals.
Running multiple model simulations with slightly varied initial conditions to assess forecast uncertainty and probability.
Sensitivity to initial conditions means small errors grow over time, limiting weather forecast reliability beyond ~10 days.
Climate pattern of Pacific Ocean temperature oscillation; El Niño (warm) and La Niña (cool) affect global weather.
A band of cold air circling the Arctic; when it weakens, it can allow frigid air to plunge into mid-latitudes.
Extended period of below-normal precipitation causing water shortages; can be worsened by higher temperatures increasing evaporation.
Prolonged period of excessive heat; exacerbated by urban heat islands, humidity, and climate change.
Field that determines how much climate change contributed to the likelihood or intensity of a specific weather event.
Conversion of liquid water to water vapor using solar energy; primary input of water to the atmosphere.
Release of water vapor from plant leaves; combined with evaporation as evapotranspiration.
Conversion of water vapor to liquid droplets; occurs when air cools to its dew point; forms clouds.
Process by which water soaks into soil and rock, replenishing groundwater supplies.
The upper surface of the zone of saturation where all pore spaces in rock/soil are filled with water.
Concentration of dissolved salts in seawater; average ~35 ppt; affects density and freezing point.
Layer of rapid temperature decrease with depth between the warm surface and cold deep ocean.
Wind-driven horizontal movement of surface ocean water; forms large circular gyres.
Global deep ocean circulation driven by differences in water density from temperature and salinity.
Large circular surface current system; five major gyres driven by winds and the Coriolis effect.
A disturbance that transfers energy through water; caused primarily by wind; water particles move in orbits.
The distance over open water that wind blows in a constant direction; longer fetch = larger waves.
Highest tidal range when Sun, Moon, and Earth align (new and full moon); gravitational forces combine.
Lowest tidal range when Sun and Moon are at right angles (quarter moons); gravitational forces partially cancel.
Current flowing parallel to the shore created by waves approaching at an angle; transports sediment along the coast.
Area of land where all precipitation drains to a common outlet (river, lake, or ocean); defined by topographic divides.
A pronounced curve or loop in a river channel formed as the river erodes its outer bank and deposits on the inner bank.
Underground layer of permeable rock or sediment that stores and transmits groundwater.
Flat area adjacent to a river that is periodically inundated during high water; built from deposited sediment.
Layering of a lake by temperature: warm surface (epilimnion), transition zone (thermocline), cold bottom (hypolimnion).
A persistent body of ice formed from compacted snow that moves under its own weight; stores ~69% of Earth's fresh water.
Continental-scale glacier covering >50,000 km²; only two exist today: Greenland and Antarctica.
Accumulation of glacial sediment (till) deposited at the edges, bottom, or terminus of a glacier.
A valley carved by glacial erosion, wider and flatter-bottomed than the V-shaped valleys carved by rivers.
When a glacier's terminus melts back because melting exceeds accumulation; accelerating globally due to climate change.
When a region uses more than 25% of its available renewable fresh water supply; affecting ~2 billion people.
Removing salt from seawater to produce fresh water; energy-intensive but increasingly important in arid regions.
The hidden water embedded in producing goods (e.g., ~15,000 liters to produce 1 kg of beef).
Total volume of fresh water used to produce goods and services consumed by an individual or community.
Ratio of water used by crops to water applied; drip irrigation (~90%) vs. flood irrigation (~50%).
Pollution from an identifiable, localized source (e.g., factory discharge pipe, sewage treatment plant).
Pollution from diffuse sources (agricultural runoff, urban stormwater); harder to control than point sources.
Excess nutrient enrichment (nitrogen, phosphorus) causing algal blooms, oxygen depletion, and aquatic die-offs.
Amount of oxygen consumed by microorganisms decomposing organic matter; high BOD indicates polluted water.
Strip of vegetation along waterways that filters pollutants, reduces erosion, and provides habitat.
1922 agreement dividing Colorado River water among 7 US states and Mexico; now over-allocated as supply declines.
Massive aquifer under the US Great Plains; supplies ~30% of US irrigation water; being depleted far faster than recharge.
Annual monsoon flooding in the Ganges-Brahmaputra delta; affects millions; worsened by climate change and sea level rise.
When legal water rights exceed actual available supply; creates conflict as water becomes scarcer.
Conservation, efficient irrigation, desalination, recycling, better allocation policy, and reducing demand.
Global ocean circulation driven by density; cold salty water sinks in North Atlantic, flows deep, upwells elsewhere; ~1,000 year cycle.
Narrow, deep, fast, warm currents on the western side of ocean basins (Gulf Stream, Kuroshio) — intensified by Coriolis and basin geometry.
Part of thermohaline circulation; transports warm surface water north and cold deep water south in the Atlantic.
f = 2Ω sin(φ); increases with latitude; determines the deflection of moving objects (including ocean currents).
Successive layers of water are deflected further from the wind direction; net transport is 90° to the wind.
